content.text: Permission modes control how much Claude Code is allowed to do
asking you first, from “ask before everything” through “read‑only planner” all the way to “no safety net, run everything.”
Every time Claude wants to use a tool — read or edit a file, run a shell command, call an MCP server, hit the network, etc. — the request goes through a permission gate. The active
tells that gate when to prompt you, when to auto‑approve, and when to refuse the action outright.
This sits on top of the allow/deny rules configured in settings and project files: the mode defines the
, while allow/deny rules provide fine‑grained overrides.
Different sources list slightly different counts, but the same core modes appear across the official docs and teaching material. At a high level you can think of five canonical modes:
Prompts for every sensitive action unless pre‑allowed
Auto‑approves file edits and safe FS ops; still prompts for shell and high‑risk tools
Focused coding when you mostly trust the context
Read‑only: Claude can analyze and propose plans, but does not execute tools
Auto‑approves (almost) all tools based on internal heuristics; minimal prompts
Disables the permission gate and runs tools without checks
Highly isolated sandboxes only, never on real machines
On the interactive TUI/CLI, you can cycle among the main interactive modes (typically
) with a single keybinding (often Shift+Tab), so changing the level of autonomy is a constant‑time operation during a session.
Regardless of mode, every tool call follows the same general flow:
Claude decides it wants to use a tool
Before touching your filesystem, shell, or network, the call is checked against deny rules, allow rules, and the current permission mode.
If the command or tool matches a deny pattern, the call is refused outright, regardless of the mode or allow rules.
If it matches an allow rule and wasn’t denied, the call is auto‑approved with no interactive prompt.
Mode decides what to do for the rest
, anything not pre‑allowed triggers an interactive prompt. - In
, file edits and safe FS ops are auto‑approved; shell and risky tools still prompt. - In
, tools are never actually executed; Claude reports what it
This layered design is why the “real” way to reduce prompt fatigue is
to jump straight to Bypass, but to use
default mode + a well‑tuned allow list + targeted deny rules
Mode-by-Mode Behavior and When to Use Each
In default mode, Claude Code prompts for every tool invocation that isn’t explicitly allowed by an allow rule.
Before editing a file, it asks.
Before running a shell command, it asks.
Before touching anything outside the project directory, it asks.
Working on unfamiliar or critical codebases.
Learning how Claude behaves on a new project.
Team environments where explicit approvals are required.
or your settings file to add allow rules so those actions run without prompts.
AcceptEdits is designed for hands‑on coding sessions where file edits are common and shell commands are rarer.
Auto‑approves file edits and common filesystem ops (
, etc.) inside the project and any additional directories you’ve configured.
Still prompts for shell commands and riskier tools unless covered by allow rules.
Focused refactoring or feature work in a mostly understood codebase.
High‑frequency “edit this file, re‑run tests occasionally” workflows, where prompts for every edit would be too noisy.
You get smoother coding with fewer interruptions but rely more heavily on deny rules and shell allowlists for safety around command execution.
Plan mode turns Claude into a
Claude can explore files, index the project, and propose a detailed step‑by‑step plan of edits and commands.
execute those edits or commands; instead, it describes what it
Architectural reviews and “what would you do?” analyses.
Safety‑critical situations where you want a plan to inspect before any change.
Teaching scenarios: use plan mode to generate a patch, inspect it, then apply it manually or by switching modes.
Ask Claude to design a refactor or migration; it produces a plan and proposed edits.
and let it execute the plan with normal prompts and protections.
Some guides describe an “auto” or “autoApprove” mode where a classifier or heuristic decides when to skip prompts.
The gate still exists, but prompts are minimized based on risk assessment.
More actions are auto‑approved, especially in well‑scoped projects and CI‑like contexts.
Deny rules remain hard stops; they still override everything.
In containerized or ephemeral environments where rollback is trivial.
For scripted, repeatable tasks where interactive prompts hurt automation.
For experimentation with a clear fallback (e.g., git reset, disposable branches).
This mode should usually be combined with strict deny rules to enforce boundaries around destructive commands (
At the far end of the spectrum is the “no guardrails” mode — often exposed as
The permission gate is effectively disabled.
All tool calls run as if they were pre‑approved, subject only to external sandboxing or OS‑level permissions.
This is explicitly marked as dangerous in official guidance and community tools.
Only in tightly sandboxed environments (e.g., throwaway containers with no real credentials, ephemeral CI runners with read‑only fixtures).
Never on a developer laptop or any environment that can touch production or personal data.
bypass is almost never the right solution to prompt fatigue
; the right pattern is default mode plus allow/deny rules.
Permission modes are only one part of the security model. A robust configuration combines them with data‑driven rules:
- Patterns for tools and commands that may run without asking. - Used to auto‑approve safe operations and reduce prompts.
- Patterns for tools and commands that must
run. - Deny entries override allow entries when both match (deny wins).
- Defines what happens for everything else. - Default: ask. AcceptEdits: auto‑approve edits. Plan: never execute. Auto/Bypass: minimal or no prompts.
The usual “expert” setup ends up being:
against local services) and directory‑scoped tool use.
, production deploys, migrations against prod DB, system‑level commands).
how often Claude must ask you
before taking actions, from fully interactive to fully autonomous.
Default, AcceptEdits, and Plan form the main “interactive” cycle for day‑to‑day work; Auto and Bypass are specialized modes for automation and heavily sandboxed environments.
Every tool call flows through the same gate: deny rules → allow rules → mode‑dependent prompt or execution.
Deny rules always win over allow rules, giving you a way to define “never do this” constraints, regardless of mode.
The recommended practice for most workflows is
default mode plus well‑tuned allow/deny rules

Permission modes and allow/deny rules are declared in Claude Code's layered settings system. Understanding which file to edit — and why — is essential for building a configuration that survives team collaboration and CI environments.
Claude Code merges settings from three sources in order, with later sources overriding earlier ones:
— applies to every project you open.
in the repo root — checked in and shared with the team.
— gitignored, for personal tweaks on top of the shared config.
key in any of these files accepts the same structure:
. The argument pattern supports shell-style glob wildcards. For tools that take multiple arguments (like
with a file path), the pattern is matched against the relevant argument.

Choosing where to put a rule determines its scope and who controls it. The wrong placement leads to either too little automation (user settings that don't follow the repo) or silent failures in CI (local settings that aren't committed).
Personal overrides on a team project

to every repo with the project's safe allow rules (test commands, linters, common build commands) and a strict deny list (production deployments, destructive commands). Each developer can then add personal overrides in
once and it stays personal across all future checkouts.
Beyond the static settings files, Claude Code's interactive TUI exposes a
command that lets you inspect and temporarily modify the active allow/deny list for the current session without editing any file.
When a permission prompt appears during a session, you are presented with options to:
— Approve this specific invocation and continue prompting for future ones.
— Add a temporary allow rule scoped to the current session only; not persisted.
to view the effective allow and deny lists at any point in a session. It shows merged rules from all sources, making it easy to diagnose why a particular tool is or isn't being prompted for.
When starting work on a new project, open with
mode and let Claude surface each new action as a prompt. After a few prompts, use
to review the session's accumulated temporary rules, then copy the safe ones into the shared
Per-Directory Allow Rules: Narrowing the Scope

Allow rules for file-system tools support glob patterns that restrict which paths Claude is permitted to touch. This is especially valuable in monorepos or projects with mixed sensitivity: you might want Claude to freely edit source code under
but prompt for every change under

In this configuration, Claude can freely read any file, edit source and test code without prompting, and run standard build/test commands — but attempts to modify infrastructure files, secrets, or run deployment tools will be denied regardless of the permission mode.
— matches any characters within a single path segment (not
— matches any number of path segments, including none.
Patterns are matched against paths relative to the project root.
Deny patterns matching the same path as allow patterns always win.

A mature Claude Code configuration for a production codebase balances developer productivity with safety. The following is a worked example for a Node.js/TypeScript monorepo with a staging and production deployment pipeline:

{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Read(**)",
      "Edit(packages/**)",
      "Edit(apps/**)",
      "Edit(*.json)",
      "Edit(*.md)",
      "Bash(npm test)",
      "Bash(npm run test:*)",
      "Bash(npm run lint)",
      "Bash(npm run build)",
      "Bash(npm run typecheck)",
      "Bash(npx tsx *)",
      "Bash(git status)",
      "Bash(git diff *)",
      "Bash(git log *)",
      "Bash(git show *)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Bash(git push*)",
      "Bash(git commit*)",
      "Bash(npm publish*)",
      "Bash(rm -rf *)",
      "Bash(*deploy*prod*)",
      "Bash(*terraform*)",
      "Bash(kubectl *)",
      "Edit(.env)",
      "Edit(.env.production)",
      "Edit(infrastructure/**)"
    ]
  }
}

Notice the structure: the allow list gives broad read access, broad edit access within application code, and a curated set of safe build and inspection commands. The deny list then carves out a strict set of operations that must never run autonomously — commits, pushes, publishing, production deployments, and files containing secrets.
mode because file edits in the application directory auto-approve, but shell operations still require per-command allow entries or prompts.
Permission Modes in Automated Pipelines and CI/CD

Claude Code can operate in non-interactive contexts — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, scripted local runners — where there is no human available to respond to permission prompts. In these environments, you need a mode and rule set that is safe without any human in the loop.
Combine with a strict deny list
that explicitly blocks the most destructive operations even in this environment.
or real API keys into the container where Claude Code runs in bypass mode.
so it cannot reach production endpoints.

# .github/workflows/claude-review.yml
jobs:
  ai-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Ephemeral container with no prod credentials
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run Claude Code analysis
        run: |
          claude --permission-mode plan \
                 --print \
                 "Review this PR for security issues and suggest fixes" \
                 > claude-review.md
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
      - name: Post review as comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const review = require('fs').readFileSync('claude-review.md', 'utf8');
            github.rest.issues.createComment({ body: review, ... });
mode for CI review — Claude analyzes and proposes but cannot actually execute changes in the CI environment.
For CI pipelines that need Claude to actually apply fixes (e.g., auto-formatting), the pattern shifts:

- name: Auto-fix linting
  run: |
    claude --permission-mode autoApprove \
           --allowedTools 'Edit,Read,Bash(npm run lint -- --fix)' \
           --print \
           "Fix all linting errors in the codebase"
  env:
    ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
to narrowly scope which tools are available, reducing the attack surface even when running in a less-restricted mode.
When Claude Code refuses to run a command you expect to work, the diagnosis process has a clear order. Understanding
a particular action was blocked requires checking all three layers of the decision system.
Step 1: Check the deny list first.

in your session and look at the deny section. A deny pattern matching the blocked command is the most common cause. Remember that deny patterns are checked before allow patterns — if a deny pattern matches, the command will be refused regardless of any allow rule.
Step 2: Check for pattern specificity mismatches.
Shell-style glob patterns in allow/deny rules can catch more than you expect. The pattern
in a deny list will block a command like
. If a deny rule is catching too broadly, narrow it (e.g.,
Step 3: Check which settings file the rule lives in.
If an allow rule is not taking effect, confirm the file is being read. A setting in
will override the same setting in
. If you added an allow rule to
has a conflicting deny rule, the deny rule wins.
mode, even commands with allow rules are not executed — plan mode prevents all tool execution regardless of other settings. Shift to
mode if you expect actual execution.
Security Threat Model for Each Mode

Choosing a permission mode is ultimately a risk management decision. Each mode represents a different position on the tradeoff between developer velocity and the blast radius of a mistake — whether that mistake is Claude misunderstanding instructions, a malicious prompt injection, or an accidental automation error.
Low — prompts give you veto power
Medium — injected content can cause unreviewed edits
Review the plan before switching modes
High — injected code can execute silently
Very High — no gate at all
Severe — full code execution with no review
Throwaway containers only, no real credentials

deserves special attention: when Claude reads files from disk, fetches web content, or processes user-provided input, that content can contain adversarial instructions designed to hijack Claude's behavior. In
mode, you get a natural checkpoint because any potentially dangerous action will surface as a permission prompt, giving you a chance to catch suspicious behavior. In
mode, injected instructions can cause real damage before you notice anything is wrong.
Trust Hierarchy: Why Deny Always Wins

One of the most important properties of the permission system is that deny rules are evaluated before allow rules, and deny always wins if both match. This is not arbitrary — it reflects a fundamental security principle:
it is always safer to fail towards restriction than towards permissiveness.

Consider what would happen if allow rules could override deny rules: a malicious prompt or a carelessly broad allow rule could silently bypass a safety constraint. By making deny unconditionally win, the system guarantees that once you have committed a deny rule to your settings file, no other configuration — not a session override, not a broad allow rule, not the mode — can override it without explicitly removing the deny rule.
Never add allow rules to "cancel out" deny rules. If you find yourself writing both
, the deny rule will win and the allow rule will be silently ignored. The right approach is to narrow the deny rule first:
outside the deny pattern if it is a command you want to permit.

This also means the deny list is your last line of defense — it is worth reviewing it carefully when setting up a new project to ensure no critical destructive operations are accidentally left unprotected.
